name: Lebanese Arabic (Beiruti)

ancestors.notes: French adstrate (Mandate-era and ongoing): heavy loanword stratum, /ʒ/ reinforced, code-switching.

notes: Lebanese Arabic — Beiruti urban koine, a South-Central Levantine variety. ق qaf → /ʔ/ glottal stop (urban; rural/Bedouin /ɡ/ via the Levantine parent). ج jim → /ʒ/. Interdentals merged in urban speech (θ→/t/~/s/, ð→/d/~/z/, ðˤ→/dˤ/). DISTINGUISHING FEATURE: STRONG imāla — Classical /aː/ raised to [eː] and final /-a(ت ة)/ raised toward [e]; short /a/ frequently fronted to [e]/[æ]. This pervasive vowel raising is the most salient Lebanese marker. French adstrate is heavy. Source: Lebanese Arabic (Wikipedia); Cowell (1964); Holes (2004).

sources.author: Cowell, M.W.
sources.title: A Reference Grammar of Syrian Arabic
sources.notes: Covers the North/South-Central Levantine continuum including Lebanese imāla.

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic
